title: Sopa de ajo
subtitle: Castilian garlic soup with bread and poached eggs
excerpt: Thinly sliced garlic fried in olive oil until golden, then simmered in chicken stock with day-old bread and smoked paprika. Eggs poached directly in the soup. A peasant dish of exceptional depth from the plateau of Castile, eaten throughout Spain in winter.
sourceNotes: Sopa de ajo is documented in Spanish cookbooks from the sixteenth century and is one of the oldest surviving Spanish dishes still cooked in the same form. It is traditionally associated with Castile and León (the high, cold plateau where the dish makes most sense) though it is eaten throughout Spain. The combination of garlic, bread, and egg in broth appears across the medieval Mediterranean as a sustaining winter food for working people.

content.text: The garlic must be cooked with care: sliced thin, fried in olive oil over medium heat until golden but not dark. Dark garlic gives the soup a bitter note that cannot be undone. Golden garlic gives it a sweet, nutty depth. The smoked paprika is added off the heat or the moment the garlic is ready, stirred quickly, and then the stock goes in immediately, paprika scorches in seconds.

Day-old bread is essential. Fresh bread dissolves into paste; stale bread absorbs the stock and swells into soft, substantial chunks. The eggs are dropped in raw and poached gently in the soup for 4 to 5 minutes, the whites set and the yolks remain soft, and when the yolk breaks into the garlic broth the soup becomes something more than the sum of its parts.
Fry the garlic and build the broth

Heat the {{olive-oil}} in a wide casserole over medium heat. Add the {{garlic}} slices and fry gently, stirring, for 4 to 5 minutes until golden, watch carefully and remove the pan from the heat the moment it starts to colour. Add the {{paprika-smoked}} and stir for 20 seconds in the residual heat. Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} immediately and bring to a simmer. Add the {{baguette}} slices and simmer for 5 minutes until the bread has swollen and softened into the broth. If using, add the {{serrano-ham}}. Season with salt and pepper.

Reduce the heat to a gentle simmer. Make four spaces in the soup and crack an {{eggs}} egg into each space. Cover and poach for 4 to 5 minutes until the whites are set and the yolks are still runny. Ladle into deep bowls, ensuring each bowl has a full egg. Scatter {{parsley-flat}} over and serve immediately.

items.fix: Start again, dark garlic cannot be saved; the slices should be pale golden, not amber or brown
items.cause: Garlic was overcooked and browned too dark
items.symptom: Garlic is bitter and the soup tastes sharp

items.fix: Use bread that is at least one day old; add it towards the end and let it absorb the stock for 5 minutes, not 15
items.cause: Too fresh, or cooked too long
items.symptom: Bread has completely dissolved into mush

items.fix: Reduce to the gentlest possible simmer before adding eggs; they should poach not buffet in the liquid
items.cause: Broth was at a rolling boil when eggs went in
items.symptom: Eggs have spread and the yolks are hard

items.fix: Use a good, well-seasoned chicken stock; the stock is the backbone of the dish, a watery stock produces a watery soup
items.cause: Stock was weak, or insufficient salt
items.symptom: Soup is bland despite the garlic

content.text: Sopa de ajo is the soup of Castile, the cold, dry plateau at the heart of Spain where winter is serious and cooking is by necessity economical and warming. It is made with the things that were always to hand: stale bread, garlic, olive oil, and whatever stock the household kept. The egg was added when available and the ham when the family could afford it. The dish has barely changed in 500 years, which is itself a demonstration of how correct the original combination is. It is still eaten for breakfast in parts of rural Castile on cold mornings, which is not the worst argument for a soup.
